role_summary: First female Prime Minister of the Brown Berets (1968-1970); co-founder of El Barrio Free Clinic (1968) and Las Adelitas de Aztlán (1970); organized logistics for the August 29, 1970 National Chicano Moratorium march; later Tongva indigenous rights activist.
ai_reading: Arellanes's top semantic neighbors are Mateo Camarillo (entry 86, count 55, the Chicano civil rights organizer from San Diego), Ben Caldwell (entry 6), Roberta Alexander (entry 110, Berkeley Black Panther Party), Maria Varela (entry 81, SNCC + Ganados del Valle), and Norma Mtume (entry 93, BPP). The cluster reads her as a Chicana-Black-cross-racial-movement voice, the embedding pulling her oral history into the orbit of both Chicano organizing (Camarillo) and Black Panther Party women (Alexander, Mtume) more strongly than into a Brown-Berets-only or East-LA-only cluster the cultural reception would predict. The cosine with Roberta Alexander and Norma Mtume is particularly notable: the embedding finds her testimony as close to two Black Panther women as to her fellow Chicano organizer Camarillo, an intersection the historiography of the Brown Berets and the BPP rarely foregrounds despite the documented organizational parallels between the two parties (the Brown Berets explicitly modeled their structure on the BPP).
biographical_paragraph: Arellanes was named Minister of Finance and Correspondence of the Brown Berets in 1968, becoming the first female Prime Minister of the organization; she co-founded the El Barrio Free Clinic on Whittier Boulevard, providing immunizations, physical exams, and drug-addiction counseling at no cost to the East Los Angeles community [src: 2]. In 1970 she co-founded Las Adelitas de Aztlán with Gracie Reyes, Hilda Reyes, Andrea and Esther Sánchez, and others, a Chicana feminist discussion and support group that operated outside the Brown Berets' male-dominated structure [src: 2]. She joined the National Chicano Moratorium Committee in 1969, marched in the February 28, 1970 "March in the Rain" carrying a white cross for a fallen cousin, and organized logistics for the August 29, 1970 march that drew 20,000-30,000 participants [src: 2]. In 1971 she opened La Clínica Familiar del Barrio on March 15, the clinic that later became AltaMed Health Services [src: 2]. Born April 26, 1946 in East Los Angeles, Arellanes died October 12, 2024, at seventy-eight [src: 2]. Her Library of Congress oral history is entry 47 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

sources.title: Gloria Arellanes oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: Mario T. Garcia, The Chicano Generation: Testimonios of the Movement
sources.publisher: University of California Press, 2015; WorldCat OCLC 889325378
